description: A14FJ - Day 6, part 2: Antares Liftoff and Rendezvous with Kitty Hawk

tokens: Al Shepard and Ed Mitchell have completed their two moonwalks and gathered a good haul of new lunar samples.
Now safely packed up in Lunar Module Antares, it will soon become spaceborne again for a rendezvous with their mothership Kitty Hawk.
New procedures are to be tried for the first time in conducting this meet-up in space, which adds some extra excitement to this crucial part of the mission.
All indications are still for a perfectly nominal lift-off.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Editor's note:All transcript times are presented according to the GET update at 054:53:36 that saw the mission timer moved forward 40 minutes, 2.90 seconds.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Al Shepard's reference to the antenna blowing over referred to the S-band erectable antenna on the lunar surface, and as on Apollo 12, when the crew fired the reaction control system jets to check them out, the thrust from those jets blew over the antenna.
We're 53 minutes now from ignition, from lunar lift off.
Progressing smoothly toward that event, about 10 minutes 25 seconds from reacquiring Kitty Hawk on its 31st revolution of the Moon.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 141 hours 37 minutes, and we're now 48 minutes, 45 seconds from lunar lift off.

The crew at this time, somewhat ahead of the Flight Plan, presently aligning the platform, on their Lunar Module guidance system, this is the stable reference number which is used by the onboard guidance system in determining spacecraft attitude.
We're about 50 seconds now from reacquiring Kitty Hawk, the Command Module in its 31st revolution of the Moon.
We're using a dual capsule communicator set up in Mission Control at this time.
Bruce McCandless is CapCom for the Lunar Module, Antares, and Ron Evans is CapCom for Stu Roosa in Kitty Hawk.
At acquisition, Evans plans to pass along to Roosa, the fact that we do not expect to have Manned Space Flight Network relay of the ascent.
Normally, when we have the high gain antenna operating properly on the Command Module, the Manned Space Flight Network would be used to relay communications from Antares to the Command Module.
This will not be done because the Command Module high gain antenna is not locking up in narrow beam width.
Roosa will get his communications from the Lunar Module, via the VHF communications circuit, which will be put into activation shortly after orbital insertion.
And we have acquisition now of Kitty Hawk.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Are you having an SOS sandwich there for lunch?
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: An SOS sandwich is a military staple food, consisting of chipped beef over toast.
'SOS' apparently stands for either 'Same old Stuff' or 'Shit on a Shingle'.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Not quite, but I sure wish it was.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: When you get a chance there, Stu, you can go ahead and terminate Bat A charge.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: And while you're floating over there, you might be advised that the TPI Delta-V will probably be - more than likely be - around 100 feet a second instead of about 90 or so, you know?
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: And your ascent PADs are good the way they are.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Antares is pressing on in good shape there.
They're getting set for the liftoff, about 39 minutes yet.
And, as you might notice here, we're on separate air-to-ground loops.
So I'll be talking to you through this pass, and I'll relay all information on up to you.
When you go to the P20 attitude, the Omni antennas just make all kinds of noise racket down here, so I may not be able to hear you, but I think you've been hearing me most of the time.
So I'll just go ahead and relay, probably in the blind, most of the time line.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, Stu, we're going to bring the probe back with us, or you guys are, anyhow.
And for TEI, just go ahead and stow it underneath the right-hand couch - you know, with the straps like we normally do, there, for temporary storage.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: - - back, we'll give you some permanent stowage instructions, during transearth coast.
Also, your VHF acquisition time - that's when you come over the horizon for the VHF there - will be 142:20:18.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: The probe has to be properly secured into the cabin.
The g forces generated by their reentry might tear it loose and cause it to slam onto equipment, the crew, or their hull.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We're now 33 minutes from lunar lift off.

Aboard the Command Module Kitty Hawk, Stu Roosa at this time is getting suited up, and CapCom Ron Evans has advised Roosa that he will be relaying on the separate air to ground link the progress of the lunar lift off.

Ed Mitchell and Al Shepard aboard Antares on the lunar surface have just completed the alignment of their guidance system platform, and CapCom Ron Evans will bepassing up to Stu Roosa some information on stowing the probe assembly in the Command Module.
The plan is to return the probe and it will be stowed under one of the couches in the Command Module Kitty Hawk.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Okay, 142:20:18; and, Ron, I'm going to be off the comm here, probably about 4 or 5 minutes.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 142 hours.
We're coming up now on 25 minutes until lunar lift off, things progressing smoothly at this time.

Mitchell and Shepard aboard Antares have switched to the guidance program, in their on board guidance system program 12 which will be used in the powered ascent from the lunar surface, and the Lunar Module ascent stage batteries, batteries 5 and 6 look good at this time.
The voltages are normally and they're sharing the load well.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: It looks like you're getting about ready to maneuver, there.
Just want to let you know the LM's in good shape.
About the only thing that's even pending is the - their steerable antenna, and it looks like it may not be tracking correctly on the thing.
So they may be using their Omni antennas.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: We'll see you on Omni Delta, here.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: You may see me; I won't see you.
Hey, by the way, that's a beautiful job whipping around out there - we got some real good selenodetic updates, and I'm sure we got a bunch of good pictures, by golly.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: At this time aboard Kitty Hawk Stu Roosa is maneuvering the Command Module to the proper attitude for the LM lift off.

He'll have the CSM apex pointed toward the lunar surface, and capcom Ron Evans has advised Roosa that the Lunar Module appears to be in good shape for lift off, with the one problem of the LM steerable antenna which may give some problem in tracking and if it does, we'll be switching to one of the omni antennas on the Lunar Module.

This is Apollo Control at 142 hours 6 minutes we're coming up now on 20 minutes until lunar lift off, and aboard Antares, Shepard and Mitchell will shortly begin pressurizing the ascent propulsion system.
Here in the control center, the large plot boards, which will be used primarily by the flight dynamics officer, and the guidance officer for the lunar ascent, has come up.
One of these displays in particular will be crucial to the flight dynamics officer, and that's the display which shows him altitude versus the rate of altitude gain.
FIDO will use this display in determining whether the LM guidance system is putting the proper amount of energy into gaining altitude as opposed to gaining velocity.

Initially the engine will be putting a larger percentage of this energy into altitude gain, later on in the burn the LM will be pitching over and the rate of altitude gain should drop off as the velocity gain begins to increase at an ever growing rate.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Antares has a Go for lift-off; direct rendezvous on this pass.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: This is Apollo Control, we're now 15 minutes from lunar lift off.
Everything looks good at this time, and we've given the crew a go for a direct ascent rendezvous with a nominal lift off time.
Our lift off time, 142 hours 25 minutes 42 seconds.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 142 hours 16 minutes, and we're coming up now on 10 minutes until lunar lift off.
it's been relatively quiet here in the control center, also relatively little communications with Antares at this time.
The powered ascent burn, scheduled to last about 7 minutes 12 seconds.
At the end of that burn we should have achieved a velocity of about 55 hundred 43 feet per second.

The electrical systems engineer, here in the control center, for the Lunar Module reports that those ascent batteries on the LM, which were a source of some concern prior to the power descent and the landing look good at this time.
This is Apollo Control, now 5 minutes from lunar lift off.
Everything still looking very good for that lift off and direct ascent rendezvous.
At the time of lift off Stu Roosa in Kitty Hawk should be about 67 nautical miles behind the Lunar Module, and I have orbit insertions on 7 minutes 12 seconds later.
The Command Module will be leading by about 135 nautical miles.
Shortly before lift off Shepard and Mitchell aboard the Lunar Module are scheduled to make a VHF voice check with Roosa in the Command Module.
We're now coming up on 4 minutes until lunar lift off.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Well, I can read you somewhat.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: And Antares, Kitty Hawk is trying to read you on VHF.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Kitty Hawk is reading you 3 by on VHF.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: And Antares is counting - counting down to 2 minutes.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell-LM

tokens: They will now switch their communications from Push to Talk mode to Voice Activated, or Vox mode.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Little less than 2 minutes; everything is Go.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: This is an entry Ed makes into the DEDA for the AGS.
Selecting400 + 10000commands the AGS to orient itself for an upcoming engine burn.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The A and B lights are on.
transcript.speaker: Shepard-LM

tokens: Al makes a reference to Address 367, which allows them to monitor the LM Altitude rate (or the H dot) on the DEDA.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Antares has got ascent engine on.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: You're coming through loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: Shepard-LM

tokens: Okay, be up to see you shortly.
transcript.speaker: Shepard-LM

tokens: Have a nice cool one set up.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell-LM

tokens: The Ascent Propulsion System generates some 3,500 pound of thrust in the lunar vacuum, and with the LM Ascent Stage's reported mass of 10,779.8 pounds, this amounts to an acceleration of 0.32 g's at liftoff.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: For the first 10 seconds, the Ascent Stage rises vertically to clear the lunar surface, before they start pitching for the purpose of starting to accelerate into orbital velocity.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: You're looking good from down here, Al.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Little bit low and slow, but PGNS are...
transcript.speaker: Mitchell-LM

tokens: FIDO says we look good, all sources.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Okay, you want to give me a 623?
transcript.speaker: Shepard-LM

tokens: Take a look at the target again.
transcript.speaker: Shepard-LM

tokens: And you're Go from down here.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: The steerable antenna tracking very well.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: VIis good, H-dot is good, H is ***, PGNS and AGS agree.
transcript.speaker: Shepard-LM

tokens: ***in oscillation in our RCS pressures, but I'm sure it's ***
transcript.speaker: Mitchell-LM

tokens: RCS looks good from down here, Ed.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: And you're Go from the ground at 3 and one-half.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Antares is still Go from the ground.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: H-dot is good; H is good, AGS are right together.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell-LM

tokens: You're Go from the ground; looking good.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: We show all sources, PGNS, AGS, and MSFN, in good agreement.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: About 225 to go, and the plane looks good.
transcript.speaker: Shepard-LM

tokens: PGNS, AGS and MSFN are all in good agreement.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Now, you can stop your camera, if you want.
transcript.speaker: Shepard-LM

tokens: We're going to be on [garble] at 5:30.
transcript.speaker: Shepard-LM

tokens: 5:30....is good H-dot's good; H is good; PGNS and AGS agree.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell-LM

tokens: FIDO says we're looking real good.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Take a look at 85 versus 500 for a minute.
transcript.speaker: Shepard-LM

tokens: Main valves Open; Ascent stage, Closed.
transcript.speaker: Shepard-LM

tokens: Roger; trim the PGNS, all axes.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: FIDO says the burn looks good.
We're shooting for a 51 by 9 orbit.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: There will be a tweak burn.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: That tweak burn will touch up the orbit to get us in the desired 51 by 9.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Are you copying the LM VHF?
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: minus 2.0; Y, plus 5.0; Z, minu 8.0;and this is it the nominal yaw 30 attitude.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: I am reading them loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: There will be a tweak burn.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Did you copy the tweak burn?
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: We're setting up for it now.
transcript.speaker: Shepard-LM

tokens: You're Go for the APS TPI.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: That tweaking over performed with the reaction control system thrusters on the LM have put the spacecraft in the proper position for the transfer phase initiation and the direct descent rendezvous.
We'll be standing by for the report from the FIDO on initiation time for the TPI burn and also the velocity required.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: You're Go for a direct rendezvous.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: I'll lock up for you as soon as we're in position.
transcript.speaker: Shepard-LM

tokens: We're locked up on the steerable.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell-LM

tokens: It is used for attitude control and for acquisition and tracking of the Command Module using the Rendezvous Radar.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Kitty Hawk, Houston; we're still checking your vector.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Okay, I was just going to start on that when I got a Go on it.
My- VHF won't stay locked on, Ron.
It'll just flash me a Range and then drop right back off again.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Okay, let me check the - You say your VHF won't stay locked on.
It locks on and then comes right back out again.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: It just flashes me a Range and then drops right back to zero.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: No state vector updates are required.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: And Stu reports he's having problems locking on in VHF.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: LM update - or LM state vector update is not required.
You're Go the way it is.
And any better luck on the VHF?
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Transmitting kind of in the blind here, because I can't read you; but go ahead and try to take the VHF monitor, and see if you get a tracking light or not.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: This is Apollo Control, at 142 hours, 43 minutes and we're now about 12 minutes 30 seconds from losing contact with the Command Module.
We'll lose contact with the LM as it goes behind the Moon about one minute earlier due to the fact that the LM is in a lower orbit than the Command Module, Kitty Hawk.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: And, Kitty Hawk; you want to be sure and make sure that the Antares remains quiet on the VHF there while we're trying to lock up on the thing.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: We believe that the Command Module VHF ranging lock problem may be due to the conversation on the loop.

Your conversation even over the intercom within the LM is enough to break it up an inhibit lock; so maybe you can get Stu to give you a mark when he's ready to throw the Ranging Reset switch, and then remain silent for about 20 seconds, both spacecraft.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: Give us a call, Stu, when you need it.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell-LM

tokens: It looks like the VHF may have - may have locked up after your Noun 49 value you had on it.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 142 hours 46 minutes and we're now about 8 minutes from loss of signal with the LM.
Stu Roosa aboard the Command Module, Kitty Hawk, now has gotten VHF lock up with the Lunar Module and has ranging to the LM and the Lunar Module is also getting range information on Kitty Hawk.

The transfer phase initiation maneuver at about 100 feet per second will be performed while the spacecraft is behind the Moon and when we reacquire they should be beginning the breaking phase on the direct approach to the Command Module and the final portion of the rendezvous and we'll begin station keeping as the spacecraft comes back around and into contact with Mission Control.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We're being informed that all systems are looking good.
In particular, BATs 5 and 6 are normal, and you're in good shape trajectory-wise going around the corner.
We've got 6 and one-half-minutes to LOS and we anticipate having a ground TPI solution for you prior to LOS.
transcript.speaker: McCandless

tokens: I lost my S-Band Antenna breaker again.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell-LM

tokens: Request Aft Omni and Lo Bit Rate:
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: And it looks like your present attitude is blocking the steerable antenna from pointing at the Earth.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Maybe so, but it also popped a circuit breaker.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell-LM

tokens: If it runs into the stop, I believe it will.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: It popped just before it went to the stop.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell-LM

tokens: As you've heard in that last exchange with Ed Mitchell on Antares, we've had a dropout of the LM steerable antenna.
We're now using the Omni antennas on the Lunar Module.
And at the present time, our data here in Mission Control shows the two spacecrafts to be about 75 miles apart.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: You've got about 5 minutes to LOS here.
And, Stu, you're accepting all the optics monitor in good shape.
You probably better go ahead and accept the VHF monitor, also.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Stu, it looks like we're getting some bad VHF spots in there.
Just reinitialize the W-matrix and go sextant only - sextant only.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Kitty Hawk, don't worry about the W-matrix on the ground.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: LM TPI ground solution, Delta-V; X plus 63 feet per second; Y plus 1; Z, plus 67.
I say again, LM TPI Delta-V: X, plus 63.0; Y, plus 1.0; Z, plus 67.0.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Looks like the best ground solution we have down there for the LM is about as written.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: Copy plus 63, plus 1, plus 67, thank you.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell-LM

tokens: See you on the other side, Ed.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: We'll see you on the other side.
transcript.speaker: Evans

tokens: We've had loss of contact now with Antares.
We'll be loosing contact with Kitty Hawk in about 45 seconds.
This is Apollo Control at 142 hours 59 minutes.
We'll be reacquiring the Lunar Module, Antares in about 43 minutes 40 seconds.
We'll pick up the Command Module a bit earlier, again because of the higher orbit that the Command Module is in, reacquiring Kitty Hawk in about 42 minutes.
By the time that we acquire, the crew will have performed the transfer phase initiation burn.
That will be about 85 feet per second, performed with the ascent propulsion system engine.

It'll be a posigrade maneuver, and as we reacquire the spacecraft, that will be on the line of sight closing, preparing to do their braking maneuvers, and shortly after reacquiring, they should begin station keeping.
At the present time, our displays predict that the separation distance between the 2 spacecraft is about 51 nautical miles, closing at a rate of about 231 feet per second.
At 143 hours this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO
